name: Cabo Verde
summary: Cabo Verde is one of Africa's most stable and advanced democracies, recently elevated to upper-middle-income status in 2025. The archipelago's economy is service-oriented, heavily reliant on tourism and remittances, and maintains a special partnership with the European Union. Despite its limited natural resources and vulnerability to climate-induced drought, Cabo Verde has achieved remarkable success in human development, including being declared malaria-free by the WHO in 2024. The nation is currently focused on economic diversification through its 'Blue Economy' and renewable energy transition.

politicalStability.note: High institutional stability; consistent peaceful transitions between major parties (MpD and PAICV); upcoming 2026 elections.

economicDirection.note: Robust growth driven by tourism surge; elevation to upper-middle-income status in 2025 reflects long-term progress.

investmentAttractiveness.note: Strong destination for FDI in West Africa due to transparent governance and EU peg; focus on eco-tourism and digital services.

geopoliticalRisk.note: Low risk of internal or external conflict; strategic partner for maritime security and counter-narcotics in the Atlantic.

educationQuality.note: High literacy rates and well-developed primary and secondary education systems.

healthcareQuality.note: Strong healthcare outcomes; officially declared malaria-free by the WHO in February 2024.

technologyInvestment.note: Advancing as a regional digital hub; significant investment in renewable energy and submarine fiber optic cables.

relationships.summary: Former colonial power and closest European partner; deep historical, cultural, and economic ties.
Key partner for maritime security and development aid (Millennium Challenge Account); large Cabo Verdean diaspora in the US.
Major partner for infrastructure development and healthcare cooperation; increasing investment in digital economy.
Important regional partner for trade, migration management, and security cooperation.

perspectives.view: The surge in visitors is creating thousands of jobs. Our future lies in high-quality, sustainable eco-tourism.
Water scarcity is our biggest challenge. Desalination powered by renewables is essential for our survival as the climate changes.
We are bringing back skills and capital from the US and Europe to invest in the digital future of our islands.
Being malaria-free is a monumental achievement that proves our health system's strength and our commitment to our people.

historicalContext.summary: Uninhabited until the 15th century, Cabo Verde was a Portuguese colony and a major hub for the transatlantic slave trade. It gained independence in 1975 and, after a period of one-party rule, successfully transitioned to a multi-party democracy in 1991. It has since become a model for democratic governance and economic development in Africa.

keyEvents.event: First multi-party elections mark the transition to democracy.
Cabo Verde graduates from the UN's Least Developed Country (LDC) status.
WHO officially declares Cabo Verde malaria-free; Secretary Blinken visits to strengthen US-CPV ties.
Cabo Verde is officially elevated to upper-middle-income status by the World Bank.

strengths: One of Africa's most stable democratic environments
Strategic maritime location and special partnership with the EU
Transparent legal system and strong protection of property rights
Malaria-free status enhancing tourism and living standards
risks: Extreme vulnerability to climate-induced drought and water scarcity
Small domestic market and high cost of inter-island transport
Heavy reliance on external economic conditions in Europe
Limited natural resources and dependence on food/energy imports
